findings.title: GppLogCard.mergeRows fully trusts the athlete copy once its length >= planned, so a coach mid-block edit/reorder silently desyncs labels
findings.evidence: mergeRows: if athlete.length >= planned.length it returns the athlete array verbatim (GppLogCard.tsx:37), only appending planned tail rows when the athlete has fewer. The re-seed effect keyed on a JSON hash of planned.rows (GppLogCard.tsx:65-70) re-runs mergeRows(planned.rows, prev) on any planned change — but because prev is the full athlete array of equal/greater length, the early-return at line 37 means the coach's edited exercise NAMES/reps for positions [0..athlete.length) are ignored; only done flags/edits the athlete made survive. Reproduction: coach renames GPP row 1 from 'Plank' to 'Side plank' (or reorders rows) after the athlete first opened the block; the athlete keeps seeing 'Plank'. This is by-position merge with last-write-wins per the comment, but a coach correction is invisible to the athlete who already viewed the block.
findings.userImpact: Coach fixes a GPP exercise name/order; athletes who already opened that block keep doing the stale version and never see the correction.
findings.recommendation: Merge per-position taking the coach's exercise/reps/sets/load as the source-of-truth fields and keeping ONLY the athlete's `done` (and any explicitly athlete-overridden value) — rather than wholesale trusting the athlete array. The plannedRowsHash effect already detects coach changes; use it to refresh the non-done fields too.

verdicts.reasoning:
The core mechanism is real and reachable. mergeRows (GppLogCard.tsx:31-39) returns the athlete array verbatim at line 37 when athlete.length >= planned.length, only ever appending planned TAIL rows (line 38) when the athlete has fewer. The re-seed effect (GppLogCard.tsx:65-70) fires on any plannedRowsHash change and calls setRows(prev => mergeRows(planned.rows, prev)); because prev is the full-length in-memory rows array (>= planned.length), line 37 returns it unchanged, so a coach's edited exercise NAME/reps/sets/load for any EXISTING position [0..athlete.length) is silently discarded. Initial seeding (line 49) hits the same path when a persisted athleteSection.rows of equal/greater length exists. I confirmed GppRow/GppSection shape in database.types.ts (166-191), that GppLogCard is the live athlete-side renderer (ExerciseLogCard.tsx:205-216), and that onSave only fires from updateRow (line 118) — there is no remount/key that would discard athlete state and re-seed clean, so the stale rows persist across reloads.

Why PARTIAL not CONFIRMED: the finding's reproduction wording ("athletes who already opened that block") is imprecise. Merely opening the block does NOT persist an athlete copy — onSave is only enqueued from updateRow (line 118), i.e. after the athlete edits or ticks at least one row. So the precondition is "athlete has touched any GPP row" (persisting a full-length metadata.gpp.rows, since the initial rows state seeded from planned.map at line 32 is already full length), after which a coach rename/reorder of an existing position is invisible. With that corrected precondition the bug is genuinely reachable both within a session (if planned data refreshes live) and, more robustly, across sessions/reloads via the line 49 initial-seed path.

Severity stays LOW: it affects only auxiliary GPP blocks; coach-ADDED rows do still surface (tail append); planned data itself is never overwritten (no data-integrity violation, only athlete-copy display staleness); and it requires the athlete to have already logged on that block before the coach edits an existing row. The recommendation (merge per-position, taking coach exercise/reps/sets/load as source-of-truth and keeping only athlete `done`) is the correct fix.
verdicts.correctedEvidence: Precondition is "athlete has edited/ticked at least one GPP row" (which persists a full-length metadata.gpp.rows via updateRow → enqueueSave at GppLogCard.tsx:115-119), NOT merely "opened the block" — onSave is never called on open. Once such a copy exists, both the initial seed (GppLogCard.tsx:49) and the plannedRowsHash re-seed (GppLogCard.tsx:66-70) hit the early return at GppLogCard.tsx:37 and discard the coach's edits to existing positions [0..athlete.length).

findings.dimensionOverview: The athlete write path is unusually careful for a mobile logger: primitive-dep effects keep mid-edit local state from being stomped on parent re-renders (SetEntryRow, RawScoreDial, GppLogCard), set rows are upserted on the stable (log_exercise_id, set_number) key with new rows numbered strictly max+1 (collision-safe across deletes/gaps), the per-row chainRef serialises a blur-then-status double-fire, GPP uses a coalescing serial queue to kill the historic duplicate-row race, ensureLogExercise tolerates legacy duplicates via order+limit(1), and runSave deliberately avoids reloading on transient network errors so it does not unmount cards mid-edit. Planned-vs-performed separation is respected everywhere (removePlannedSet/GPP/substitution all write to training_log_*, never the plan). Those are genuine strengths. However there are several REAL reachable problems where the athlete loses data or gets stuck: (1) the single-slot pending-set-delete drops the previous delete when a second set is deleted inside the 4s undo window — the first set is gone from the UI but never deleted server-side, OR the reverse depending on timing; (2) the free_text_reps dead card with no parsable "x" leaves an exercise with zero rows and no way to mark it done; (3) every onBlur-only textarea (session notes, exercise notes, GPP exercise-name) silently loses the last edit if the app is closed/backgrounded without blurring; (4) the bonus-day-delete reads a stale `overview` closure and double-sets dayIndex; (5) read-modify-write of session.custom_metrics and exercise.metadata is non-atomic, so concurrent metric/GPP writes can clobber keys; (6) save failures surface only as a small red banner the athlete will not see while scrolled down a long session, with no per-field retry. Findings below are ordered by severity.

findings.title: VAS=0 (no pain) is visually indistinguishable from 'not entered' until the athlete reads the tiny readout
findings.evidence: The range input renders value={local ?? 0} (VasField.tsx:55), so a null/un-entered VAS shows the thumb pinned at 0 — identical to a deliberately logged 0. Only the small numeric readout distinguishes them ('–' vs '0', VasField.tsx:65). Since 0 is a meaningful value (no pain) and the most common honest answer, an athlete glancing at the slider can't tell whether they've recorded 'no pain' or simply not touched it. The header doc-comment also claims 0.5-step support for coaches (VasField.tsx:5-8) but step is hardcoded to 1 (line 53) — a latent contradiction.
findings.userImpact: Athlete may skip VAS thinking they already set it (slider already at 0), or set 0 and not be sure it registered.
findings.recommendation: Render the slider in a visibly 'unset' state when local==null (e.g. dim the track / show a placeholder thumb) distinct from a logged 0, and reconcile the doc-comment's 0.5-step claim with the actual step=1.

verdicts.reasoning:
Read the full VasField.tsx (79 lines) plus the coach-side VAS config in WeekMetricsSettings.tsx. Two sub-claims, mixed results.

Sub-claim 1 (visual ambiguity) — REAL but overstated. Line 55 value={local ?? 0} does pin the range thumb at position 0 for both an un-entered null and a deliberately logged 0; a native range input has no 'null' position, so it falls back to min=0 (line 52). That slider-thumb ambiguity is reachable. HOWEVER the evidence's claim that 'only the small numeric readout distinguishes them' is inaccurate: there are TWO distinguishing signals, not one. (a) The numeric readout at lines 62-66 is text-lg font-bold (not tiny) and renders '0' in emerald-300 for a logged 0 vs '–' in gray-600 for null. (b) The 'clear' button (lines 67-75) renders only when local != null, so it is absent for an un-entered field and present once 0 is logged — a second, independent cue. So the slider-position ambiguity holds, but the readout is neither uniquely-distinguishing nor especially tiny.

Sub-claim 2 (doc-comment contradiction) — REFUTED. The header comment (lines 5-7) does NOT claim 0.5-step slider support. It reads: 'Stored as numeric so a coach who treats it as 0.5-step can do so, but the slider snaps to integers since that's what athletes report in practice.' That explicitly says the slider uses integer steps, which is exactly what step={1} (line 54) implements. No contradiction. I also checked the coach VAS config (WeekMetricsSettings.tsx:71,79,311-313) — it is a plain on/off trackVas toggle labeled 'VAS pain (0–10)', with no step/0.5 configuration anywhere, so the comment's 0.5 reference is purely about numeric storage, consistent with the slider snapping to integers.

Net: the underlying UX nit (null vs logged-0 share the same thumb position) is genuine and severity nit is right, but the cited evidence is partly wrong — the '0.5-step contradiction' does not exist and the 'only the tiny readout' framing understates the existing affordances (colored bold readout + conditional clear button).
verdicts.correctedEvidence: VasField.tsx:55 value={local ?? 0} pins the range thumb at 0 for both null and a logged 0 (native range has no null state). The distinction is conveyed by TWO cues, not one: (1) the bold text-lg readout '0'/emerald vs '–'/gray-600 (lines 62-66), and (2) the 'clear' button that only renders when local != null (lines 67-75). The doc-comment 0.5-step claim is NOT a contradiction: lines 5-7 say storage is numeric 'but the slider snaps to integers', matching step={1} on line 54.

findings.title: UndoToast auto-dismiss timer re-arms on every parent re-render because onDismiss is a fresh closure each render
findings.evidence: UndoToast's effect depends on [visible, onDismiss, timeoutMs] (UndoToast.tsx:54). onDismiss is an inline arrow recreated on every TodayScreen render (TodayScreen.tsx:1152). While the toast is visible, any unrelated re-render of TodayScreen (e.g. a `saving` flip from another in-flight save, a metric merge) gives a new onDismiss identity → the effect cleanup clears the timer and re-arms a fresh 4000ms timeout. A session where background activity keeps re-rendering can therefore keep RESETTING the undo window so it never elapses — the pending delete is never committed (the set stays in limbo, removed from UI but not from DB) until renders quiesce. Conversely a render storm right at the 4s mark can delay the commit indefinitely.
findings.userImpact: Edge case, but a deleted set can sit un-committed far longer than the intended 4s, and the eventual commit timing is non-deterministic; combined with finding ATHLETE-ROBUSTNESS-1 it widens the window for lost/abandoned deletes.
findings.recommendation: Wrap the TodayScreen onDismiss/onUndo handlers in useCallback (stable identity), or have UndoToast capture onDismiss in a ref and depend only on [visible, timeoutMs] so the timer arms once per visibility transition.

verdicts.reasoning:
The core mechanism is real and the cited lines are accurate. UndoToast.tsx:46-54 arms its auto-dismiss timer in a useEffect with deps [visible, onDismiss, timeoutMs], and TodayScreen.tsx:1152-1160 passes onDismiss as an inline arrow recreated on every render. React behavior is exactly as described: any re-render of TodayScreen while the toast is visible gives onDismiss a new identity, so the effect cleanup (UndoToast.tsx:52) clears the pending timer and re-arms a fresh timeoutMs window. The optimistic-removal/limbo framing is also accurate — the set is removed from UI at TodayScreen.tsx:684-698 and only committed via deleteLoggedSet inside onDismiss at 1157-1158, so a re-armed timer genuinely delays the DB commit. The recommended fix (useCallback or a ref capturing onDismiss, depending only on [visible, timeoutMs]) is correct.

Where the finding overreaches on reachability: I grepped TodayScreen for any autonomous re-render source and found NONE — no setInterval, no polling, no realtime/onSnapshot subscriptions. The only effects (lines 213-243) are dependency-driven (loadWeek/loadDay, markMessagesRead on mode change, URL-param consume, mode reset on day change). runSave (278-301) only flips `saving` true→false around an explicitly user-initiated action. Therefore the catastrophic claims — "background activity keeps re-rendering", "render storm", "never elapses", "delay the commit indefinitely" — are NOT substantiated by the real code. In a single-user athlete mobile session there is no stream of concurrent in-flight saves completing every <4s on their own.

The actually-reachable manifestation is narrower and benign: if the athlete performs another logging action (set save, metric edit, field toggle — each runs runSave → 2 re-renders) within the 4s undo window, the timer is cancelled and re-armed, extending the window and making the destructive commit's timing non-deterministic. The pending delete is preserved in pendingSetDelete state across re-renders, so nothing is lost while the screen stays mounted; the commit still fires once renders quiesce. This justifies a real-but-minor verdict. Severity low is appropriate (borderline nit); marking partial because the bug is genuine while the stated user impact is overstated.
verdicts.correctedEvidence: Mechanism confirmed at UndoToast.tsx:46-54 (effect deps include the unstable onDismiss) and TodayScreen.tsx:1152 (inline arrow). Reachable trigger is a user-initiated runSave during the 4s window (saving true→false re-render at TodayScreen.tsx:279/299), NOT autonomous background activity — TodayScreen has no intervals/polling/subscriptions. Effect: undo window extended past 4s and commit timing non-deterministic; the delete is not lost (pendingSetDelete persists in state, optimistic UI removal at 684-698, commit at 1157-1158).

findings.title: Coach editing any set nulls the athlete's performed_text (combo tuple) and notes — silent data loss
findings.evidence: upsertLoggedSet builds a FULL row object and `.upsert(row, { onConflict: 'log_exercise_id,set_number' })` (trainingLogService.ts:739-742) — this is a whole-row replace, not a partial patch. The row sets `performed_text: patch.performedText ?? null` (line 733) and `notes: patch.notes ?? null` (line 736). CoachSetEditModal.saveRow (lines 104-115) calls upsertLoggedSet passing ONLY logExerciseId, setNumber, plannedLoad, plannedReps, performedLoad, performedReps, rpe, status — it never passes performedText or notes. Repro: athlete logs a combo set as reps '2+2+2' (stored in performed_text per StackedNotation.tsx:276-277 / SetEntryRow), or completes a free-text exercise whose prose lives in performed_text; coach opens the Pencil → CoachSetEditModal and blurs ANY field (even just confirming the load). EditableRow.commit (CoachSetEditModal.tsx:251-257) fires saveRow → upsert writes performed_text=null. The tuple '2+2+2' is destroyed and the Did row collapses to the numeric sum (or '?'), and any per-set athlete note is wiped.
findings.userImpact: A coach making a routine correction (e.g. fixing one set's kg) silently erases the athlete's recorded combo tuple notation and any per-set note on every row they touch. The coach then reviews a degraded record and the athlete's actual input is gone — a direct violation of 'athlete input is stored separately and compliance is derived by comparison, not mutation', here the coach review tool itself mutates/destroys logged athlete data.
findings.recommendation: In CoachSetEditModal.saveRow, carry the existing row.performed_text and row.notes through to upsertLoggedSet (e.g. `performedText: row.performed_text ?? null, notes: row.notes ?? null`). The row already spreads the full TrainingLogSet in the useEffect mapping (CoachSetEditModal.tsx:66-69) so the values are available. Longer-term, make upsertLoggedSet patch-only (read-modify-write or column-scoped update) so omitted fields are never clobbered — the same whole-row upsert risk applies to any future caller.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the real code; the data-loss path is fully reachable.

1) Whole-row replace, not a patch. trainingLogService.ts:725-746 — upsertLoggedSet builds a complete row literal and does `.upsert(row, { onConflict: 'log_exercise_id,set_number' })`. On conflict (existing set) this replaces ALL columns. Crucially the row hardcodes `performed_text: patch.performedText ?? null` (line 733) and `notes: patch.notes ?? null` (line 736). Any caller that omits these fields writes NULL over whatever was there.

2) Coach modal omits both fields. CoachSetEditModal.saveRow (lines 104-115) constructs the SetPatch with only logExerciseId, setNumber, plannedLoad, plannedReps, performedLoad, performedReps, rpe:null, status — it never forwards performedText or notes. The full TrainingLogSet IS available in `row` (mapped via `{ ...s, ... }` at lines 66-69), so the omission is purely the bug, not a data-availability limit.

3) The clobbered data is real, current, athlete-authored. SetEntryRow.tsx:142-156 stores combo/tuple reps like "2+2+2" into performed_text (line 147-148: `trimmedReps.includes('+') ? trimmedReps : ...`), and free-text-mode prose into performed_text (line 125). StackedNotation.tsx:271-282 renders `s.performed_text ?? ...` as the reps display in the coach review surface, collapsing to the numeric sum or 'x'/'?' once it's nulled. So a coach correction silently destroys the athlete's recorded tuple — exactly as described.

4) The trigger is reachable on routine actions. LogModeView.tsx:334-349 mounts CoachSetEditModal with real `editingLogged.sets` (live logged data). EditableRow.commit (lines 251-257) fires on blur of the load OR reps input (onBlur at lines 308, 324) and on Enter; cycleDone/cycleSkipped (lines 259-264) fire on the ✓/✗ toggles. ALL route through saveRow → whole-row upsert. So merely toggling a set's status or confirming any field wipes performed_text on that row.

Adjustment to the notes sub-claim (does not lower severity): per-exercise athlete notes now live in training_log_exercises.performed_notes (TodayScreen.tsx:619-663), a different table not touched by upsertLoggedSet, and current free-text athlete input goes to performed_text rather than the set-level `notes` column. The set-level `notes` clobber therefore only hits LEGACY "pre-A3" rows (the fallback at SetEntryRow.tsx:64-71, 88 explicitly reads `logged?.notes`), which the upsert still nulls. So the notes-loss portion is real but legacy/edge; the performed_text loss is fully current, reachable, and silent. Net verdict: confirmed; critical stands because routine coach edits irreversibly destroy live athlete-logged combo data and violate the "athlete input stored separately, never mutated" principle. The recommendation (carry row.performed_text/row.notes through, or make upsertLoggedSet patch-only) is correct.
verdicts.correctedEvidence: Core path confirmed: trainingLogService.ts:733 (performed_text nulled), :736 (notes nulled), :739-742 (whole-row upsert on conflict); CoachSetEditModal.tsx:106-115 (saveRow omits performedText/notes) and :251-264 (commit/cycleDone/cycleSkipped triggers); athlete writes performed_text at SetEntryRow.tsx:125,147-156; rendered at StackedNotation.tsx:276-277; modal mounted with live sets at LogModeView.tsx:334-339. Caveat: set-level `notes` loss applies to legacy "pre-A3" rows only (current athlete free-text uses performed_text; per-exercise notes use training_log_exercises.performed_notes, a separate table). performed_text loss is fully current and reachable.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified end-to-end and could not refute it.

ROOT CAUSE (confirmed): upsertLoggedSet (trainingLogService.ts:725-747) builds a FULL row literal that explicitly sets performed_text: patch.performedText ?? null (line 733) and notes: patch.notes ?? null (line 736), then `.upsert(row, { onConflict: 'log_exercise_id,set_number' })` (line 742). Because the keys are present with explicit null values (not omitted from the object), the ON CONFLICT update writes those columns to NULL — this is a genuine whole-row replace, not a PostgREST partial-key merge. CoachSetEditModal.saveRow (CoachSetEditModal.tsx:104-115) passes only logExerciseId, setNumber, planned*, performed{Load,Reps}, rpe:null, status — never performedText or notes. So any save nulls them.

DATA AT RISK (confirmed): Combo tuples like "2+2+2" are written by the athlete into performed_text (SetEntryRow.tsx:142-156: `trimmedReps.includes('+') ? trimmedReps : ...`), and current free-text prose for off-plan/extra rows is also written there (SetEntryRow.tsx:118-126). StackedNotation reads performed_text first for the reps display (StackedNotation.tsx:276-282) and falls back to the numeric sum or '?'/'x', so after a coach save the tuple visibly collapses. Repro is solid.

TRIGGER IS BROADER THAN STATED (finding understated, not inflated): the evidence cites only EditableRow.commit on blur (lines 251-257), but cycleDone/cycleSkipped (lines 259-264) also call saveRow. A coach merely clicking the ✓/✗ status toggle — without touching any field — nulls performed_text and notes. That strengthens, not weakens, the severity.

ONE CORRECTION on the `notes` claim: live athlete per-set notes do NOT currently flow into training_log_sets.notes — athlete exercise notes go to training_log_exercises.performed_notes (TodayScreen.tsx:624,663). However set-level `notes` is a documented legacy/fallback store for pre-A3 free-text and extra-row prose, and SetEntryRow still READS logged?.notes as a fallback (SetEntryRow.tsx:71,88; comments lines 30,46,64-65,117). Carrying them through (performedText: row.performed_text ?? null, notes: row.notes ?? null) faithfully preserves logged athlete data and cannot break other prescription types — for brand-new local rows row.performed_text is undefined → ?? null → null, which is the correct default. The longer-term patch-only/column-scoped-update recommendation is also consistent with the planned-vs-logged separation principle and protects future callers. No design-token or athlete-dark-theme concerns apply (this is a coach data-write bug). CorrectedEvidence reflects the broader trigger and the notes nuance.

findings.dimensionOverview: Coach Log mode is structurally sound for the clean numeric path (absolute_kg): Plan/Did StackedNotation, per-cell deltas, the RAW pillar matrix, the off-plan/substitution chips, the destructive-confirm flows, and the optimistic comment append are all correct and respect planned-vs-logged separation (nothing in the read/summary path mutates planned data). However the review side faithfully mirrors — and in two places amplifies — the athlete-side type problems, and it has its own correctness defects. The most serious is CoachSetEditModal silently nulling performed_text and notes on every set edit (data loss for combos and prose completions). A close second is a dead GPP-summary code path (an always-undefined exercise_code cast) that makes all GPP work count as 0/0 in the per-exercise strip and Day-total rollup. Beyond those: free_text / "other" / rpe exercises render misleading "Sets —/1 Reps —/0" compliance noise; percentage exercises compare a % planned number against performed kg under a "kg" label; the Sessions stat and Days-trained dots under-report any fully-logged-but-not-"Finished" session; exercise-scoped comment threads are read-only on the coach side; GroupLogView calls supabase directly (API-first violation); and three date/time formatters violate the European DD/MM + 24h convention. Findings below are ordered by severity.

findings.title: GPP summary path is dead (always-undefined exercise_code cast) — GPP work counts as 0/0 in per-exercise strip and Day total
findings.evidence: logSummary.computeExerciseSummary detects GPP via `getSentinelType((planned as PlannedExercise & { exercise_code?: string | null }).exercise_code ?? null)` (logSummary.ts:169). But PlannedExercise (database.types.ts:202-223) has NO exercise_code field — it lives only on the joined Exercise (database.types.ts:93). The cast resolves to undefined → `undefined ?? null` → getSentinelType(null) returns null (sentinelUtils.ts:51-56) → the gpp branch (logSummary.ts:170-174 calling the correct gppSummary at 92-111) NEVER fires. GPP then flows through the generic path: plannedExerciseTotals reads summary_*=0 and GPP has no prescription_raw, so it returns sets/reps=null; actualSets=countCompletedSets(logged.sets)=0 because GPP stores done flags in metadata.gpp, never training_log_sets. Net: every GPP exercise contributes 0/null to LogDayCard's Day-total rollup (LogDayCard.tsx:147 maps every planned through computeExerciseSummary). Note LogExerciseRow's own GPP table renders correctly because it reads planned.exercise.exercise_code (LogExerciseRow.tsx:69), so the leak is invisible per-row but real in the Day total.
findings.userImpact: A day built around GPP/circuit work (or a day total that includes a GPP block) under-reports the athlete's volume: the coach's 'Day total Sets/Reps' rollup ignores all GPP rows, so a session that was largely GPP can read as near-zero training even when the athlete ticked every row done.
findings.recommendation: Read the joined Exercise's code: `getSentinelType(planned?.exercise?.exercise_code ?? null)` — but computeExerciseSummary takes a bare PlannedExercise, so either widen its signature to accept the joined `(PlannedExercise & { exercise: Exercise })` (callers already pass that — LogDayCard.tsx:147, LogExerciseRow.tsx:218) or pass exercise_code explicitly. Then gppSummary will populate the rollup from the GPP rows.

verdicts.reasoning: The core bug is real and reachable. (1) PlannedExercise (database.types.ts:202-223) has NO own exercise_code field; exercise_code lives only on the joined Exercise (database.types.ts:93). (2) At runtime the objects flowing into computeExerciseSummary are PlannedExercise & { exercise: Exercise } (LogDayCard.tsx:24, mapped at LogDayCard.tsx:147; LogExerciseRow props at LogExerciseRow.tsx:26). (3) logSummary.ts:169 reads the TOP-LEVEL `.exercise_code` via the cast `(planned as PlannedExercise & { exercise_code?: string | null }).exercise_code`, but the value lives at `.exercise.exercise_code`. So it resolves to undefined → `undefined ?? null` → getSentinelType(null) (sentinelUtils.ts:51-56) returns null → the `sentinel === 'gpp'` branch (logSummary.ts:170-174 → gppSummary at 92-111) NEVER fires. Contrast LogExerciseRow.tsx:69, which correctly reads planned.exercise.exercise_code. (4) GPP then takes the generic path: GPP exercises store rows/done flags in metadata.gpp, never in training_log_sets (confirmed by setLogExerciseGppSection at trainingLogService.ts:655-675, which writes metadata.gpp). They have prescription_raw=null and unpopulated summary_* (sentinelService.ts:30-49 creates the GPP exercise with default_unit 'other', counts_towards_totals false; the planner stores GPP only in metadata, never prescription_raw). So plannedExerciseTotals (logSummary.ts:128-160) skips its recompute fallback (line 136 requires truthy prescription_raw) and returns planned sets/reps = null; countCompletedSets(logged.sets)=0 and sumPerformedReps=0 because the sets array is empty. Result: GPP contributes planned=null (skipped by computeDaySummary's sumMetric at logSummary.ts:218-225) and actual=0 to the Day-total rollup — i.e. all GPP volume is silently dropped from LogDayCard's Day total (LogDayCard.tsx:151-160). The fix the reviewer proposes (read planned.exercise.exercise_code, widening the signature as LogExerciseRow.tsx:69 already does) is correct. ONE INACCURACY in the title/evidence: the claim that GPP also reads 0/0 in the "per-exercise strip" is wrong — LogExerciseRow short-circuits into its own GPP branch (LogExerciseRow.tsx:113-216) BEFORE calling computeExerciseSummary at LogExerciseRow.tsx:218, so the per-row GPP table renders correctly from planned.exercise.exercise_code. The defect manifests ONLY in the Day-total rollup, not the per-exercise strip. verdicts.correctedEvidence: Bug is real at logSummary.ts:169 (top-level exercise_code cast resolves to undefined at runtime; value lives at planned.exercise.exercise_code per database.types.ts:93 and LogExerciseRow.tsx:69). verdicts.reasoning:
The core bug is REAL and reachable. PlannedExercise (database.types.ts:202-223) has no exercise_code field — the only exercise_code declaration in database.types.ts is line 93, on Exercise. So the cast at logSummary.ts:169, `(planned as PlannedExercise & { exercise_code?: string | null }).exercise_code`, reads a property that does not exist at runtime. LogDayCard.tsx:147 passes `ex` typed `PlannedExercise & { exercise: Exercise }`, where the real code is at `ex.exercise.exercise_code`, not top-level. So `exercise_code` resolves to undefined → `undefined ?? null` → getSentinelType(null) returns null (sentinelUtils.ts:51-56) → the gpp branch (logSummary.ts:170-174 → gppSummary at 92-111) NEVER fires for the Day-total rollup. GPP then flows through the generic path: GPP sentinels carry no prescription_raw (content is in metadata.gpp; DayEditor.tsx confirms sentinels are excluded from summary_* and never get those fields populated), so plannedExerciseTotals (logSummary.ts:128-160) returns sets/reps=null (cache 0/0 + no prescription_raw skips the recompute at line 136), and countCompletedSets reads training_log_sets which GPP never writes (trainingLogModel.ts:78 confirms "GPP exercises have no set rows"), so actual=0. In computeDaySummary, null planned contributes nothing and 0 actual contributes 0 — GPP is entirely absent from the Day-total Sets/Reps rollup at LogDayCard.tsx:157-158. Confirmed.

Two reasons for PARTIAL rather than confirmed-at-high:
(1) The title overclaims "per-exercise strip AND Day total." LogExerciseRow returns early at the gpp branch (LogExerciseRow.tsx:113-216) and renders its own correct GPP table (doneCount/displayRows.length, reading planned.exercise.exercise_code at line 69); the generic computeExerciseSummary call at LogExerciseRow.tsx:218 and its PlanActual strip (317-322) are NEVER reached for GPP. So the per-exercise strip is NOT affected — only the Day total. The finding body actually concedes this ("invisible per-row but real in the Day total"), but the title is wrong.
(2) Severity/impact is inflated. gppSummary (logSummary.ts:108-109) deliberately returns null avgLoad/maxLoad, so even when fixed it only contributes Sets/Reps for accessory/circuit work — the barbell Avg/Max load axes are unaffected either way. A typical OWL day is mostly barbell work that counts correctly, so the Day total is only materially wrong on a day that is predominantly GPP. The userImpact "near-zero training" is the worst case, not the norm. This is a genuine coach-facing rollup correctness bug, but its blast radius (one rollup line, accessory volume only) puts it at medium, not high.

The recommendation is SOUND and EMOS-consistent: reading the canonical code from the joined Exercise (`getSentinelType(planned?.exercise?.exercise_code ?? null)`) honours single-source-of-truth and cannot break other prescription types (the gpp branch only fires for the GPP sentinel; absolute_kg/percentage/rpe/free_text/combo all keep flowing through the generic path unchanged). findings.dimensionOverview: Coach Log mode is structurally sound for the clean numeric path (absolute_kg): Plan/Did StackedNotation, per-cell deltas, the RAW pillar matrix, the off-plan/substitution chips, the destructive-confirm flows, and the optimistic comment append are all correct and respect planned-vs-logged separation (nothing in the read/summary path mutates planned data). However the review side faithfully mirrors — and in two places amplifies — the athlete-side type problems, and it has its own correctness defects. The most serious is CoachSetEditModal silently nulling performed_text and notes on every set edit (data loss for combos and prose completions). A close second is a dead GPP-summary code path (an always-undefined exercise_code cast) that makes all GPP work count as 0/0 in the per-exercise strip and Day-total rollup. Beyond those: free_text / "other" / rpe exercises render misleading "Sets —/1 Reps —/0" compliance noise; percentage exercises compare a % planned number against performed kg under a "kg" label; the Sessions stat and Days-trained dots under-report any fully-logged-but-not-"Finished" session; exercise-scoped comment threads are read-only on the coach side; GroupLogView calls supabase directly (API-first violation); and three date/time formatters violate the European DD/MM + 24h convention. Findings below are ordered by severity.

findings.title: free_text / 'other' / rpe exercises render misleading 'Sets —/1 Reps —/0' compliance noise on the coach side
findings.evidence: For a free_text/'other' prescription the athlete's ✓ persists a status-only training_log_set (completed, load/reps null). On the coach side LogExerciseRow always renders the PlanActual strip when `logged || planned` (LogExerciseRow.tsx:316). computeExerciseSummary: plannedSets/plannedReps resolve to null (free_text has no set lines and computePrescriptionSummary returns 0/0 so plannedExerciseTotals falls through to null — logSummary.ts:154-159); actualSets=countCompletedSets=1, actualReps=sumPerformedReps=0 (null performed_reps → 0). So the strip shows 'Sets —/1  Reps —/0  Avg —/—  Max —/—'. toneFor (logSummary.ts:40-49) tints 'Sets —/1' neutral (planned<=0 && actual>0) and 'Reps —/0' pending-grey — i.e. a black '1' and a grey '0' presented as compliance numbers for an accept/reject-only exercise. rpe is worse: with no set lines the athlete card is a dead/empty card so nothing logs, but if set lines exist (legacy) the RPE value is treated as a kg load.
findings.userImpact: For note-style and accept/reject exercises the coach sees a quantified-looking 'Sets —/1 Reps —/0' that conveys no real training and visually contradicts the gracefully-rendered '✓ Done' Did row right above it. It clutters the review and, summed into the Day total, slightly inflates the day's Sets count by 1 per such exercise with 0 reps — a confusing, semi-meaningless rollup.
findings.recommendation: When the exercise is non-quantified (unit free_text/'other', or the Did row resolved to the allNonNumeric branch in LoggedStackedNotation), suppress the PlanActual strip entirely (the '✓ Done'/'Skipped'/prose Did row is the complete signal). This mirrors how sentinel rows already render no Plan/Did stack. Tie it to the same unit check used elsewhere rather than re-deriving.

verdicts.reasoning:
The core mechanism is real and reachable, but the finding's cited rendered string is wrong.

CONFIRMED chain:
1. Athlete side: for unit=free_text/other, ExerciseLogCard synthesises a single freeTextMode planned row (ExerciseLogCard.tsx:125-135, isFreeTextRow has freeTextPlanned !== undefined). Tapping ✓ calls SetEntryRow.commit with the freeTextMode branch (SetEntryRow.tsx:114-126), persisting performedLoad:null, performedReps:null, status:'completed', performedText:null. So a status-only completed set with null load/reps is created. Confirmed.
2. Coach side: free_text/other/rpe are NOT sentinels (getSentinelType keys on exercise_code TEXT/IMAGE/VIDEO/GPP, not unit — sentinelUtils.ts:51-57), so they fall through to the main row render. The PlanActual strip renders whenever (logged || planned) (LogExerciseRow.tsx:316). Confirmed reachable.
3. computeExerciseSummary (logSummary.ts:193-201): actualSets = countCompletedSets = 1; actualReps = sumPerformedReps = (null ?? 0) = 0; loadStats over null loads → avg/max null. Confirmed.
4. toneFor (logSummary.ts:40-49): Sets pair (planned 0, actual 1) → planned<=0 && actual>0 → neutral (black 1); Reps pair (planned 0, actual 0) → planned<=0 && actual not>0 → pending (grey 0). Tones match the finding.
5. Day-total inflation: computeDaySummary sumMetric(s=>s.sets) adds actual=1 per such exercise (logSummary.ts:211-232). Confirmed real.

REFUTED detail (why partial): the finding's evidence says the strip shows 'Sets —/1 Reps —/0', i.e. planned rendered as '—' (null). It does NOT. writePrescription always writes summary_total_sets:0 / summary_total_reps:0 for free_text/other via computePrescriptionSummary returning {0,0,null,null} (useWeekPlans.ts:503-511, 529-543; prescriptionParser.ts:382-404). plannedExerciseTotals (logSummary.ts:128-160) sees cachedSets=0,cachedReps=0, recomputes 0/0 (not >0), falls through to `summary_total_sets ?? null` = 0 (the ?? does NOT fire because the value is 0, not null/undefined). So planned resolves to 0, and PlanActual.format(0,0)='0' (PlanActual.tsx:30-33). The real on-screen strip is 'Sets 0/1  Reps 0/0  Avg —/—  Max —/—', not 'Sets —/1 Reps —/0'. A '—' planned only occurs for legacy rows whose summary columns are literally NULL — an edge case, not the mainline the finding describes.

So the user-visible problem (a meaningless quantified-looking 'Sets 0/1 Reps 0/0' for an accept/reject-only exercise, contradicting the '✓ Done' Did row, plus +1 inflation in the day Sets rollup) is genuine and the recommendation (suppress the PlanActual strip for non-quantified units, mirroring sentinel rows) is sound. But because the specific rendered string in the evidence is incorrect and the visual noise is '0' rather than the more alarming '—' (and Avg/Max already collapse to '—/—'), the severity is medium, not high: it is review clutter / minor rollup distortion, not a correctness or data-integrity bug. Planned data is never mutated, the athlete signal is intact, and Avg/Max already render gracefully.

Secondary rpe claim is directionally correct but separate: rpe with no set lines yields rows.length===0 → 'No set lines defined for this exercise' dead card (ExerciseLogCard.tsx:332-334), and legacy rpe rows that did persist numeric set lines treat the RPE figure as load_value/kg — but that is a different defect from the free_text PlanActual strip and is only asserted, not the focus of the cited lines.
verdicts.correctedEvidence: Coach-side strip for a completed free_text/other exercise actually renders 'Sets 0/1  Reps 0/0  Avg —/—  Max —/—' (LogExerciseRow.tsx:316-322 + PlanActual.tsx:35-49), NOT 'Sets —/1 Reps —/0'. Planned resolves to 0 (not null): writePrescription stores summary_total_sets:0/summary_total_reps:0 for free_text/other (useWeekPlans.ts:507-508 via prescriptionParser.ts:382-404), and plannedExerciseTotals' `summary_total_sets ?? null` keeps the 0 (logSummary.ts:154-159). Real, reachable noise + day Sets actual inflated by 1 (logSummary.ts:193-201, 211-232); the '—' planned only appears for legacy NULL-summary rows.

verdicts.reasoning:
The core bug is real and reachable. For unit=free_text/'other', ExerciseLogCard.tsx:125-135 synthesizes exactly one freeTextMode row; tapping the ✓ commits status-only via SetEntryRow.tsx:114-126 + setDone (170-174): performedLoad=null, performedReps=null, status='completed', performedText=null. LogExerciseRow.tsx:316 renders the PlanActual strip whenever (logged||planned) with no exclusion for non-quantified units (only TEXT/IMAGE/VIDEO/GPP sentinels return early at lines 71-216). computeExerciseSummary then gives actualSets=countCompletedSets=1 (logSummary.ts:77,193) and actualReps=sumPerformedReps=0 (null→0, lines 67-73,194). So the strip shows quantified-looking Sets/Reps directly under a "Did" row that the allNonNumeric branch (StackedNotation.tsx:227-260) already renders gracefully as "✓ Done" — a genuine self-contradiction. The day rollup at LogDayCard.tsx:147-151 feeds every summary into computeDaySummary, whose sumMetric (logSummary.ts:211-232) adds the actual=1 set (0 reps) into the Day total — confirming the +1-set-per-note-exercise inflation. The fix (suppress the strip for non-quantified units, mirroring the sentinel early-return, tied to a shared unit check like isFreeTextUnit in ExerciseLogCard.tsx:157) is sound: it touches only display, mutates no planned/logged data, and does not affect absolute_kg/percentage/free_text_reps/combo rows which legitimately quantify — low cross-type risk.

Two corrections drop it to partial/medium. (1) Evidence imprecision: the finding asserts plannedSets/plannedReps resolve to null (rendering "Sets —/1 Reps —/0"). In the normal path they resolve to 0, not null: useWeekPlans.ts:507-508 persists summary_total_sets/reps = computePrescriptionSummary() = 0 for free_text (prescriptionParser.ts:382-404, with parseFreeTextPrescription returning [] for pure prose, lines 172-210), and plannedExerciseTotals falls through to 0 ?? null = 0 (logSummary.ts:154-159). toneFor's planned<=0 branch (logSummary.ts:44) still yields the described neutral "1" / pending grey "0" tones, so the noise is real, but the planned glyph is "0" (not "—") except on legacy/copy paths where the cache is genuinely null. (2) Severity: this is a coach-review display+rollup nuisance — no data corruption, planned data untouched, athlete UX fine. The only concrete correctness harm is the day-total off-by-one (1 set, 0 reps per note exercise), which is small and arguably defensible. "high" is inflated; medium reflects the real but cosmetic/minor-rollup impact.
verdicts.correctedEvidence: Normal-path render is "Sets 0/1  Reps 0/0  Avg —/—  Max —/—" (planned side = 0, not "—"): summary_total_sets/reps are persisted as 0 for free_text via useWeekPlans.ts:507-508 (computePrescriptionSummary = 0, prescriptionParser.ts:382-404), and plannedExerciseTotals returns 0 ?? null = 0 (logSummary.ts:154-159). The "—" planned glyph only appears on legacy/copy paths where summary_total_sets is null. Day-total inflation confirmed at LogDayCard.tsx:147-151 + computeDaySummary sumMetric (logSummary.ts:211-232): +1 actual set, 0 reps per ticked free_text/'other' exercise.

findings.title: Percentage exercises compare planned % against performed kg under a 'kg' label in Avg/Max
findings.evidence: For unit='percentage', planned_set_lines store the bare percent number in load_value, so summary_avg_load / summary_highest_load are percent numbers (e.g. 80). computeExerciseSummary copies those into avgLoad.planned / maxLoad.planned (logSummary.ts:157-158, 200-201). LogExerciseRow renders `<PlanActual label='Avg' metric={summary.avgLoad} unit='kg' decimals={0} />` and the same for Max (lines 320-321). The athlete's performed kg goes into actual. Result: 'Avg 80 / 102 kg' — planned is 80% rendered as if 80 kg, against 102 actual kg; toneFor computes 102/80=1.27 → neutral tint, treating it as 'met'. The week-overview tonnage correctly skips non-absolute_kg (LogWeekOverview.tsx:61) but the per-row Avg/Max strip does not.
findings.userImpact: For percentage-based programming the coach's per-exercise Avg/Max kg comparison is nonsense: an 80% prescription shows '80 / <kg> kg' and is mis-tinted as on-target. The coach cannot trust the Avg/Max axes for any non-kg unit, undermining the at-a-glance review.
findings.recommendation: Suppress (render '—') the Avg/Max planned side for any unit other than absolute_kg, matching the tonnage gate already in LogWeekOverview.tsx:61. The planned Sets/Reps pair stays valid; only the load axes are unit-sensitive. Drive it off `planned.unit === 'absolute_kg'`.

verdicts.reasoning:
Traced the full chain and confirmed the bug is reachable at runtime for unit='percentage'.

WRITE PATH: useWeekPlans.ts:503-510 persists summary_avg_load/summary_highest_load straight from computePrescriptionSummary. For unit='percentage', computePrescriptionSummary (prescriptionParser.ts:382-398) is NOT text-based, NOT non-numeric, NOT free_text_reps, so it takes the numeric parsePrescription branch (line 391). parsePrescription strips '%' (line 61) and parses the bare number, so '80%×3×5' yields highest_load=80 and avg_load=80 — the percent value, not kg. This is corroborated by the engine test fixture (engine.test.ts:59): unit:'percentage', prescription_raw:'80%×3×3', summary_highest_load:80, summary_avg_load:80.

SUMMARY PATH: plannedExerciseTotals (logSummary.ts:154-159) returns avg/max = summary_avg_load/summary_highest_load (the 80) with no unit gate. computeExerciseSummary copies these into avgLoad.planned / maxLoad.planned (lines 200-201) — again, no unit check anywhere except the GPP sentinel branch (line 170).

RENDER PATH: LogExerciseRow.tsx:320-321 renders both metrics with unit="kg". The athlete logs performed_load in kg (SetEntryRow.tsx:69, kg cell at :257), so .actual is real kg. Result is literally 'Avg 80 / 102 kg'.

TONE: toneFor (logSummary.ts:40-49) with planned=80, actual=102 → ratio 1.275 ≥ 0.95 → 'neutral' (no tint), i.e. mis-tinted as on-target. Even if the athlete under-lifts the percent number absurdly, the comparison is meaningless because units differ.

The recommendation's precedent is real: LogWeekOverview.tsx:61 already gates tonnage on unit==='absolute_kg'. The per-row Avg/Max strip has no such gate.

One scope correction (does not change severity): the finding says the coach 'cannot trust the Avg/Max axes for ANY non-kg unit', but rpe and free_text/free_text_reps already render '—' because computePrescriptionSummary returns highest_load:null/avg_load:null for them (lines 399-404). The bug is specific to unit='percentage' (and interval-percentage). So the fix correctly keyed on planned.unit === 'absolute_kg' is right, but the only currently-broken unit is percentage. Medium is appropriate: it silently mislabels percentage-program reviews but does not corrupt data.
verdicts.correctedEvidence: Bug manifests specifically for unit='percentage' (and interval percentage). Write path: useWeekPlans.ts:503-510 persists computePrescriptionSummary output; for percentage that is the bare percent (parser strips '%', prescriptionParser.ts:61, numeric branch :391). logSummary.ts:157-158 (cache return) and 200-201 (copy into avgLoad/maxLoad planned) carry it with no unit gate; LogExerciseRow.tsx:320-321 labels it 'kg'; toneFor (logSummary.ts:44-48) mis-tints 102kg/80% = neutral. rpe/free_text/free_text_reps already render '—' (computePrescriptionSummary returns null loads, :399-404), so they are NOT affected — contrary to the finding's 'any non-kg unit' phrasing.

findings.title: Sessions stat and Days-trained dots under-report any fully-logged session the athlete didn't tap 'Finish session' on
findings.evidence: Sessions are created status='pending' (trainingLogService.ts:498) and only promoted to 'completed' when the athlete explicitly taps 'Finish session' (TodayScreen.tsx:1041-1049). Exercises auto-promote to completed (TodayScreen.tsx:558-563) but the SESSION status does not. LogWeekOverview.completedSessions counts only `session?.status === 'completed'` (lines 140-142) and the Days-trained strip uses `session?.status ?? 'pending'` (line 157). Repro: athlete logs every set on every exercise for a day but closes the app without tapping Finish → session stays pending/in_progress → coach sees the Sessions stat as e.g. '2/3' and a grey/amber day dot for a fully-trained day.
findings.userImpact: The coach's headline 'Sessions performed/planned' and the day-dot roster systematically under-count training for athletes who log diligently but don't ritually tap Finish — exactly the at-a-glance compliance number a coach relies on. It reads as a missed/incomplete session when the work was done.
findings.recommendation: Derive a 'logged enough' signal independent of the explicit Finish action — e.g. count a session as trained when it has ≥1 completed exercise, or surface a distinct 'logged but not finished' dot state — so the coach distinguishes 'no work' from 'work done, not formally finished'. Keep the explicit 'completed' state too, but don't let it be the sole truth for compliance.

verdicts.reasoning: All cited code paths verified against the real source. (1) Sessions are inserted with status='pending' (trainingLogService.ts:498). (2) The session is promoted to 'completed' ONLY by handleFinishSession (TodayScreen.tsx:831-832), which is wired solely to the explicit "Finish session" button (TodayScreen.tsx:1041-1049). (3) handleSaveSet (TodayScreen.tsx:527-566) auto-promotes the EXERCISE to 'completed' when all planned sets reach a terminal state (lines 555-563) but never touches the session status; it only calls getOrCreateSession/mergeSession. (4) updateSession in the service (trainingLogService.ts:531-544) is a pure passthrough with no status side-effect. (5) The coach's "Sessions" headline counts only status==='completed' (LogWeekOverview.tsx:140-142, rendered at 225-230) and the Days-trained strip uses status ?? 'pending' (lines 157, 163) feeding DayDot, where 'pending' renders a grey dot (STATUS_DOT, line 297). So an athlete who logs every set on every exercise but closes the app without tapping Finish leaves the session at 'pending' → coach sees an under-counted Sessions ratio and a grey "no work" dot for a fully-trained day. The bug is reachable and manifests exactly as described. One evidence correction: the finding says the dot would be "grey/amber" and references an in_progress state, but a grep of src/athlete/v2 found NO use of 'in_progress' — the athlete app never sets that status, so the dot is always GREY (pending), never amber, for this scenario. The amber STATUS_DOT entry (LogWeekOverview.tsx:298) is effectively dead for athlete-driven sessions. This is a minor wording fix; the core under-reporting and misleading-grey-dot impact is real. Severity medium is appropriate: it is a systematic mislead of the coach's at-a-glance compliance number, but it is cosmetic/derived (no data loss; the underlying per-exercise completion data is intact and correct).
verdicts.correctedEvidence: Repro confirmed via trainingLogService.ts:498 (insert status='pending'), TodayScreen.tsx:831-832 + 1041-1049 (session→completed only on explicit Finish), TodayScreen.tsx:555-563 (exercise auto-promotes, session does NOT), LogWeekOverview.tsx:140-142 (Sessions counts only 'completed'), LogWeekOverview.tsx:157/163 + 296-307 (DayDot renders 'pending' as grey). Correction: no 'in_progress' status is ever set in src/athlete/v2 (grep: no matches), so the under-reported day shows a GREY 'pending' dot, never amber — the amber STATUS_DOT case is dead for athlete sessions.

findings.title: Exercise-scoped comment threads are read-only on the coach side (badge counts them, no composer)
findings.evidence: LogExerciseRow renders a per-exercise comment COUNT badge filtered on `m.exercise_id === logged.log.id` (LogExerciseRow.tsx:45-47, 251-256), so an athlete can attach a comment to a specific exercise and the coach sees the count. But LogModeView only wires session-level posting: postSessionComment hard-codes `exerciseId: null` (LogModeView.tsx:138-141) and the only composer is the session thread in LogDayCard (LogDayCard.tsx:224-246, filtered to `!m.exercise_id` at line 86). There is no UI to open or reply to an exercise-scoped thread, even though addComment supports exerciseId (trainingLogService.ts:984-1000).
findings.userImpact: If an athlete asks a question on a specific exercise, the coach sees only a count badge with no way to read the thread or reply in-context. The coach must reply at the session level, breaking the per-exercise conversation the data model supports — a dead-end for targeted coaching feedback.
findings.recommendation: Make the exercise comment badge open an inline LogCommentsThread (compact mode already exists, LogCommentsThread.tsx:16) scoped to that exercise, with a post handler that passes exerciseId. findings.title: Date/time formatters in coach Log mode violate the mandated European DD/MM + 24h convention
findings.evidence: CLAUDE.md mandates DD/MM dates and 24h time, formatted via dateUtils.ts. LogDayCard's performedLabel uses `new Date(...).toLocaleDateString(undefined, { weekday:'short', month:'short', day:'numeric' })` (LogDayCard.tsx:90-93) → 'Jun 10' (US month-first, locale-dependent). LogModeView's 'loaded' stamp uses `toLocaleTimeString([], { hour:'2-digit', minute:'2-digit', second:'2-digit' })` (LogModeView.tsx:232) → '2:30:01 PM' on en-US. formatTimestamp in the shared comment thread uses `toLocaleString(undefined, { month:'short', day:'numeric', hour:'2-digit', minute:'2-digit' })` (logFormatUtils.ts:12-20) → 'May 20, 2:30 PM'. Canonical formatters (formatDateShort → DD/MM, formatDateToDDMMYYYY) already exist (dateUtils.ts:16,25).
findings.userImpact: Coaches reviewing logs see US-style 'Jun 10' / '2:30 PM' dates and times that contradict the rest of the app's European DD/MM 24h presentation, and which silently flip format with the viewer's OS locale — an inconsistency and a correctness risk for day-first readers (10/06 vs 06/10).
findings.recommendation: Route all three through dateUtils (formatDateShort for the day label) and a 24h time formatter (HH:mm). For the comment timestamp, add/extend a shared DD/MM HH:mm helper in dateUtils so both coach and athlete threads comply. Do not pass `undefined` locale to toLocale* in user-facing strings.

verdicts.reasoning: All three cited code paths verified verbatim and confirmed reachable/rendered in coach Log mode. (1) LogDayCard.tsx:90-93 builds performedLabel via new Date(performedDate+'T00:00:00').toLocaleDateString(undefined, { weekday:'short', month:'short', day:'numeric' }); it is rendered at LogDayCard.tsx:113-115 ("logged {performedLabel}") in the day-card header whenever session.date exists → "Mon, Jun 10" style, US month-first and OS-locale-dependent. (2) LogModeView.tsx:232 renders loadedAt.toLocaleTimeString([], { hour:'2-digit', minute:'2-digit', second:'2-digit' }) in the header strip whenever a log is loaded (loadedAt state set on load, line 60) → "2:30:01 PM" on en-US, 12h. (3) logFormatUtils.ts:12-20 formatTimestamp uses toLocaleString(undefined, { month:'short', day:'numeric', hour:'2-digit', minute:'2-digit' }) → "Jun 10, 2:30 PM"; it is consumed by LogCommentsThread.tsx:66 (rendered from coach-facing LogDayCard.tsx:239) and also AthleteCommentsThread.tsx:69. All contradict the CLAUDE.md mandate (DD/MM dates, 24h time, formatted via dateUtils.ts). Canonical European formatters already exist: dateUtils.ts:25 formatDateShort → DD/MM, dateUtils.ts:16 formatDateToDDMMYYYY → DD/MM/YYYY, but neither is used here. The undefined/[] locale makes output silently flip with the viewer's OS locale, creating day-first ambiguity (10/06 vs 06/10) for the European target users. This is a real, reachable consistency/correctness issue, not theoretical. Severity medium is correct: cosmetic/consistency rather than data corruption, but the day-first ambiguity is a genuine correctness risk. Minor note: formatTimestamp is shared, so the same violation also manifests in the athlete app (AthleteCommentsThread.tsx:69) — the recommendation to fix the shared helper for both surfaces is sound. Line citations are accurate; LogDayCard cite "89-94" precisely brackets the performedLabel block (def at 90-93).

findings.title: GroupLogView calls supabase directly from a presentational component (API-first violation)
findings.evidence: GroupLogView imports `supabase` (GroupLogView.tsx:17) and runs an inline `supabase.from('week_plans').select('athlete_id').eq('source_group_plan_id', ...).eq('week_start', ...)` query in a useEffect (lines 53-66) to determine sync status. EMOS principle 2 requires all log data access to go through the service layer; this is the only Log-mode component that hits supabase directly from presentation. Every other read here goes through trainingLogService (LogModeView.tsx:17-26).
findings.userImpact: No user-visible bug, but the sync-status query duplicates query logic outside the typed service layer, so a schema change to week_plans linkage won't be caught centrally and the component can't be unit-tested without a live client.
findings.recommendation: Move the sync-status query into a typed helper in trainingLogService (e.g. fetchGroupSyncStatus(groupPlanId, weekStart)) and consume it from GroupLogView, matching the rest of the Log module.

verdicts.reasoning: All cited claims verified against the real code. GroupLogView.tsx:17 imports `supabase` from lib/supabase, and a Grep across src/components/planner/log confirms it is the ONLY file in the Log module that imports supabase directly. The inline sync-status query runs in a useEffect at GroupLogView.tsx:53-58 (`supabase.from('week_plans').select('athlete_id').eq('source_group_plan_id', weekPlan.id).eq('week_start', weekStart).not('athlete_id','is',null)`), with result handling through line 67 — the finding's cited range 52-66 accurately covers this. GroupLogView is genuinely presentational: WeeklyPlanner.tsx:1506 renders it inline in Log mode, passing props (group, weekPlan, weekStart, onSelectAthlete); it only renders a roster banner + per-athlete buttons. Meanwhile LogModeView.tsx:17-26 routes ALL its data access through trainingLogService (fetchWeekLog, addComment, fetchWeekMetricsConfig, etc.), confirming the established API-first pattern. The query logic itself is NOT centralized: `source_group_plan_id` appears nowhere in src/lib except database.types.ts:151 (a type field), and trainingLogService's 5 `supabase.from(...)` calls do not include any sync-status helper. So this is a real, reachable EMOS-principle-2 (API-first / no direct supabase in presentational components) violation — the useEffect fires on mount whenever a group plan is opened in Log mode. Reachability is trivially satisfied. Severity correctly stays low: there is no user-visible bug, only a testability/maintainability concern (schema-change drift, can't unit-test without a live client), exactly as the finding states. The recommendation to extract a typed fetchGroupSyncStatus(groupPlanId, weekStart) into trainingLogService is sound and consistent with the rest of the module.

findings.title: Text custom-metric values are hard-truncated to 14 chars in the only coach review surface
findings.evidence: OtherMetricsTable renders a text custom metric cell as `String(v).slice(0, 14)` (LogWeekOverview.tsx:588) with the full value only in the cell `title` tooltip (line 578). There is no other coach-side render of custom-metric values (not on the LogDayCard glance row, not in SessionPreview). A text metric like 'left shoulder twinge during snatch' shows as 'left shoulder ' inline.
findings.userImpact: A coach scanning a free-text custom metric (e.g. a pain-area description) sees a truncated fragment and must hover each cell to read the actual value — the metric they configured is effectively unreadable at a glance, the opposite of the dense scan-friendly review the table is for.
findings.recommendation: Allow text metric cells to wrap (the table is already in an overflow-x container) or widen/word-break instead of a 14-char hard cut; if space is tight, show first line + an explicit expand affordance rather than a silent slice. Also apply the project's comma-decimal convention to the numeric custom/avg formatting (currently toFixed(1) with a period, LogWeekOverview.tsx:592).

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the real code. Text custom metrics are a genuine coach-configurable type: AthleteMetricDefinition.value_type is 'number' | 'text' (database.types.ts:405), and CustomMetricEntry carries value_text for text metrics (database.types.ts:368-370). In OtherMetricsTable, a definition with value_type !== 'number' is pushed with numeric:false (LogWeekOverview.tsx:514,524-530) and its text value flows into row.values via the value_text branch (LogWeekOverview.tsx:521). The render at LogWeekOverview.tsx:580-588 then hits the else branch `String(v).slice(0, 14)` (line 588) — a hard 14-char cut with no ellipsis, no truncate/whitespace-nowrap class, and the full value only in the cell title tooltip (line 578: title={`${row.label}: ${v}`}). The worked example is accurate: "left shoulder twinge during snatch" → "left shoulder " (14 chars). 

Reachability of the alternate-surface claim is also substantiated: value_text appears in only three files repo-wide — LogWeekOverview.tsx (this coach surface), database.types.ts (types), and src/athlete/v2/components/CustomMetricField.tsx (athlete-side INPUT, not a coach review surface). LogDayCard renders only bodyweight on its glance row (LogDayCard.tsx:126-127), not custom-metric values; SessionPreview lives in the athlete app (src/athlete/v2/). So OtherMetricsTable is indeed the only coach-side render of custom-metric values. The data path is fully reachable at runtime — this is a real bug, not theoretical.

The secondary recommendation is also valid: numeric custom/avg formatting uses toFixed(1) with a period (LogWeekOverview.tsx:586 and :592), which contradicts the project's comma-decimal convention for user-facing numerics.

Severity 'low' is honest: this degrades at-a-glance readability of the dense review table the user cares about, and forces per-cell hovering, but it is not data loss or a functional break — the value is stored intact and recoverable via tooltip. The condition (text metric + value >14 chars, e.g. a pain-area note) is plausible and easy to trigger.

findings.title: Substituted exercise is compared against the original plan's loads — deltas can mislead with no caveat
findings.evidence: When the athlete substitutes (e.g. Snatch → Power Snatch) the log keeps planned_exercise_id, so computeExerciseSummary still pulls the ORIGINAL planned summary_* and the Plan StackedNotation still shows the original prescription (LogExerciseRow.tsx:286-290). The PlanActual Avg/Max/Sets/Reps deltas therefore compare a different exercise's performance against the planned exercise's targets. The substitution IS clearly chipped ('⇄ for <planned>', lines 233-240), but the quantitative strip carries no caveat.
findings.userImpact: The coach sees a green/amber/red compliance tint comparing, say, Power Snatch kg against the planned Snatch kg/% — a delta that may look like under-performance when the athlete simply did a different lift. The visual chip warns about the swap but the numbers next to it still imply a like-for-like comparison.
findings.recommendation: When isSubstituted, dim or annotate the PlanActual load axes (or show the planned targets as reference-only) so the coach isn't nudged by a misleading delta tint. The Sets/Reps comparison is more defensible than the load comparison; consider scoping the caveat to Avg/Max.

verdicts.reasoning:
I tried to refute this by tracing the substitution data flow end-to-end and could not. Every link in the chain is real and reachable:

1. setSubstitutedExercise (src/lib/trainingLogService.ts:762-774) updates ONLY exercise_id on the existing training_log_exercises row and explicitly keeps planned_exercise_id intact (the comment at lines 757-760 states this is the intended athlete UX, e.g. "Snatch → Power Snatch").

2. LogDayCard buckets logged exercises: any row with a planned_exercise_id goes into loggedByPlannedId (src/components/planner/log/LogDayCard.tsx:74-80), NOT into offPlan. So a substituted exercise is matched to its planned slot at line 172 (loggedByPlannedId.get(ex.id)) and passed to LogExerciseRow with BOTH planned (original) and logged (substituted) props (lines 171-192). It is NOT treated as "Added by athlete".

3. LogExerciseRow detects substitution (src/components/planner/log/LogExerciseRow.tsx:53-56), renders the swap chip (lines 233-240), but the Plan StackedNotation still renders the ORIGINAL planned.prescription_raw / planned.unit (lines 286-290).

4. computeExerciseSummary(planned, logged) (logSummary.ts:162-203, called at LogExerciseRow.tsx:218) pulls planned targets via plannedExerciseTotals(planned) (lines 176-178) = the original exercise's summary_avg_load / summary_highest_load, then compares the substituted exercise's logged loadStats against them (lines 197-202).

5. PlanActual (src/components/planner/log/PlanActual.tsx:35-47) applies toneFor() (logSummary.ts:40-49, ratio = actual/planned) to tint the Avg and Max load cells amber/red, with no substitution awareness. Rendered at LogExerciseRow.tsx:318-321.

I confirmed the load axes carry real values in the realistic case: for unit=absolute_kg the planner populates summary_avg_load/summary_highest_load (computePrescriptionSummary, prescriptionParser.ts:391-397), so a lighter substituted lift (e.g. Power Snatch) WILL produce a sub-1.0 ratio and an amber/red tint against the heavier planned Snatch — a misleading like-for-like delta exactly as described.

So the bug manifests, but its blast radius is genuinely small and self-mitigating: the swap is prominently chipped right next to the strip (lines 233-240), so a coach has the context to interpret the tint; the impact is "a tint may nudge the reader" rather than data corruption or a dead/unusable card. Also note the finding's own caveat holds — for percentage/rpe/free_text units the planned load axes are null and render "—", so no misleading load delta occurs; the issue is scoped to numeric (absolute_kg) and combo prescriptions. Sets/Reps comparisons remain broadly defensible. Severity "low" is correct. All cited line ranges (LogExerciseRow.tsx:53-63, 286-322; logSummary.ts:162-202) are accurate.

findings.title: Hand-rolled bg-blue-600 Send button in the coach comment composer (off-token chrome)
findings.evidence: The coach-facing comment Send button uses raw `bg-blue-600 ... hover:bg-blue-500 disabled:bg-gray-300` (LogCommentsThread.tsx:96) instead of the shared Button primitive / accent token. CLAUDE.md: never hand-roll bg-blue-600 buttons; use `<Button iconOnly variant=... icon={Send} />` and the accent token (#185FA5, not blue-600 #2563EB). The surrounding Log components otherwise use design tokens (e.g. GroupLogView).
findings.userImpact: Minor off-brand tell on a coach surface — the comment send button reads as generic blue, slightly inconsistent with the EMOS accent used elsewhere; no functional impact.

Same applies to the Pencil/Trash icon buttons in LogExerciseRow which hand-roll hover:text-blue-600 / hover:text-red-600 instead of Button iconOnly ghost/danger.

verdicts.reasoning: Read the cited file directly. LogCommentsThread.tsx:96 (inside the 93-100 range) renders a raw <button> with className "...bg-blue-600 text-white hover:bg-blue-500 disabled:bg-gray-300..." — a hand-rolled off-token button, not the shared Button primitive nor var(--color-accent). The button is in the coach comment composer (placeholder "Comment as coach…", onPost), which renders unconditionally in the component return, so the code path is plainly reachable on the coach-review (light-token) surface — this is not athlete dark-theme intentional Tailwind. findings.title: DB column defaults (track_raw/track_bodyweight = false) contradict the app's no-config fallback (RAW + BW on)
findings.evidence: The migration declares `track_raw boolean not null default false`, `track_bodyweight ... default false`, `track_vas ... default false` (migration lines 60-62). But the application's no-config-row fallback is the OPPOSITE for two of them: SessionHeader.tsx:91-92 sets `showRaw = metricsConfig ? track_raw : true` and `showBw = ... : true`; the same true/true/false default is re-encoded in DEFAULT_PANEL (WeekMetricsSettings.tsx:76-81) and again in LogWeekOverview.tsx:186-188. So the intended product default is RAW-on + BW-on, but the DB would materialise RAW-off + BW-off for any row inserted without explicitly listing those columns. Today the only insert path (upsertWeekMetricsConfig, trainingLogService.ts:1647-1651) always sends explicit booleans seeded from DEFAULT_PANEL, so the contradiction is currently masked. It becomes a live bug the moment a config row is created by any path that does not set these columns — a future migration backfill, a SQL seed, a group-template copy, or an `enabled_custom_metric_ids`-only insert. The same default is duplicated in 4 source locations plus the migration; if any one drifts, athlete and coach disagree on what is tracked (CLAUDE.md principle #3, single source of truth).
findings.userImpact: Latent: a config row born from anything other than the popover (or a single drifting copy of the default constant) silently stops showing RAW and Bodyweight to the athlete while the coach still believes they are default-on, so wellbeing data quietly stops being collected with no visible error.
findings.recommendation: Make the DB defaults match the product intent (`track_raw default true`, `track_bodyweight default true`) so an under-specified insert is safe, OR collapse the four duplicated app-side defaults into one exported constant (e.g. METRIC_TRACKING_DEFAULTS in trainingLogModel.ts) consumed by SessionHeader, WeekMetricsSettings, and LogWeekOverview. Prefer doing both: align the migration default and dedupe the constant.

verdicts.reasoning:
Every cited fact is accurate. Migration 20260519000002:60-62 declares track_raw/track_bodyweight/track_vas all `not null default false`. The app's no-config fallback is the opposite for RAW and BW: SessionHeader.tsx:91-93 (`showRaw = metricsConfig ? track_raw : true`, `showBw = ... : true`, `showVas = ... : false`), and the same true/true/false default is re-encoded in DEFAULT_PANEL (WeekMetricsSettings.tsx:76-81) and LogWeekOverview.tsx:186-188. So the product intent (RAW-on + BW-on by default) genuinely contradicts the DB column default (RAW-off + BW-off). The four-way duplication of the default constant is also real and is a legitimate single-source-of-truth concern (CLAUDE.md #3).

However the REACHABILITY lens does not substantiate a live bug. The DB default is dead because the column is `not null` and the sole insert path always supplies a value.

Downgraded to low: a latent consistency/maintainability issue with no current user impact. The recommendation (align migration default to true and/or dedupe into one exported constant) is sound and cheap.

findings.title: VAS promises 0.5-step / 1-decimal precision but the athlete slider hardcodes integer steps
findings.evidence: VasField's own doc-comment says "Stored as numeric so a coach who treats it as 0.5-step can do so, but the slider snaps to integers" (VasField.tsx:5-8), and the migration comment claims it is "Stored as numeric so coaches can use 1-decimal precision if they want" (migration:14-15). But the only input control is `<input type=range min=0 max=10 step={1}>` (VasField.tsx:54) and the change handler does `parseInt(e.target.value, 10)` (VasField.tsx:30), which discards any fractional value. There is no coach-facing VAS entry control at all (grep confirms VasField only renders in the athlete app), so the documented half-step/decimal capability is unreachable by anyone. The column genuinely is `numeric` (migration:17), so the data model supports it; only the UI forecloses it.
findings.userImpact: An athlete (or coach) who wants to report 6.5/10 pain cannot — the slider snaps to whole numbers, contradicting the in-code promise. Low data-fidelity for a pain metric where half-points are clinically meaningful.
findings.recommendation: Either deliver the documented precision (set `step={0.5}` and switch the handler to `parseNumericInput`/`parseFloat`, formatting the readout with a comma decimal per EU convention) or remove the misleading comments so the contract matches the integer-only reality. Given EMOS's expert-tool stance, half-step VAS is the better fix.

verdicts.reasoning: All four cited lines are verbatim accurate. VasField.tsx:5-8 doc-comment claims "Stored as numeric so a coach who treats it as 0.5-step can do so, but the slider snaps to integers." VasField.tsx:54 is `step={1}` and VasField.tsx:30 is `parseInt(e.target.value, 10)`, so fractional values are impossible. The migration column training_log_sessions.vas_score really is `numeric null` (migration:17) and migration:14-15 comment promises "coaches can use 1-decimal precision if they want." I verified the reachability claim: grep across the whole repo shows VasField is the ONLY VAS input control — it is imported and rendered only in the athlete app (SessionHeader.tsx:135, gated by showVas). The coach side never offers a VAS entry control: LogWeekOverview.tsx:509 merely READS weekLog[...]?.session?.vas_score into a read-only week-overview row, and WeekMetricsSettings.tsx only toggles track_vas on/off. So the documented half-step/decimal capability is genuinely unreachable by anyone through the UI — the data model supports it but no input path does. The inconsistency is real and reachable. I downgrade severity from medium to low: this is a documentation-vs-implementation contract mismatch with low real-world impact (the athlete flow works correctly and only collects integers, which the VasField comment itself states is "what athletes report in practice"). The genuinely misleading text is the migration comment overstating decimal capability; the userImpact framing ("athlete cannot report 6.5") is accurate but the clinical-significance argument is mild for an expert OWL tool, not a functional defect.

Confirmed there is no coach-facing VAS input: LogWeekOverview.tsx:509 only reads vas_score for display; WeekMetricsSettings.tsx only toggles track_vas.

findings.title: Null VAS is visually identical to a logged 0 (no-pain) — slider thumb defaults to 0
findings.evidence: The slider renders `value={local ?? 0}` (VasField.tsx:55), so when no VAS has been entered the thumb sits at the far-left 0 position — the exact same position as a deliberately-logged 0 (no pain). The only disambiguator is the tiny numeric readout, which shows '–' for null vs '0' for a logged zero (VasField.tsx:62-66). 0 is a fully legitimate VAS value (no pain), so an athlete glancing at the rail cannot tell "I have not answered" from "I answered zero", and may assume it is pre-filled and skip it. On the coach side this is fine (OtherMetricsTable renders '—' for null vs '0' for a logged zero, LogWeekOverview.tsx:580-588), so the ambiguity is purely the athlete entry control.
findings.userImpact: Athletes can mistake an un-answered VAS for a logged 0, under-reporting pain; or conversely never realise the field is awaiting input because the thumb already looks 'set'.
findings.recommendation: When `local == null`, visually mark the track as un-set — e.g. render the thumb in a muted/empty state, dim the rail, or show an explicit 'Not set — tap to rate' affordance, only colouring the thumb once a value is committed. Keep 0 fully selectable as a distinct, meaningful value.

verdicts.reasoning: All cited code matches the real source and the path is reachable. VasField.tsx:55 renders the range input as value={local ?? 0}, so when no VAS has been entered the thumb sits at the far-left 0 — visually identical to a deliberately-logged 0. VasField.tsx:62-66 confirms the ONLY disambiguator is the small numeric readout (text muted gray-600 + '–' for null vs a coloured digit for a value); the slider thumb/track itself carries no un-set affordance. The un-set state is genuinely reachable, not theoretical: SessionHeader.tsx:135 passes value={session?.vas_score ?? null}, so a session whose VAS has never been touched feeds null straight into the control. 0 is a documented, fully legitimate value (VasField.tsx:6 'no pain'; in-UI legend '0 none · 10 worst' at line 47), so null-vs-0 is a real semantic collision. The coach-side asymmetry the finding relies on also checks out: LogWeekOverview.tsx:580-588 renders '—' for null but the literal value (incl. 0) when logged, so the coach overview distinguishes them and the ambiguity is isolated to the athlete entry slider. This is a real perception/affordance bug, but it is not data corruption — the stored value is correct and the numeric readout offers a (weak) disambiguator — so medium severity is right; it could even be argued low, but the under-reporting-of-pain risk and the 'looks pre-filled, skip it' failure mode justify keeping it at medium.

findings.title: Text custom-metric values are hard-truncated to 14 chars in the only coach review surface
findings.evidence: A text custom metric is entered by the athlete in a 2-row textarea with no length limit (CustomMetricField.tsx:62-71). The ONLY place a coach can read it back is the OtherMetricsTable, which renders `String(v).slice(0, 14)` (LogWeekOverview.tsx:588) — hard-truncated to 14 characters, left-aligned amber cells with no Avg. The full value is only in the cell `title` tooltip (LogWeekOverview.tsx:578), which is hover-only and inaccessible on touch. There is no per-day (LogDayCard header) or SessionPreview surface for custom metrics either, so 14 chars in a wide week table is the coach's only at-a-glance view. The example labels the popover suggests ('Pain area', 'Hydration', WeekMetricsSettings.tsx:334) are exactly the free-text notes that exceed 14 chars (e.g. 'left shoulder, front delt' → 'left shoulder,').
findings.userImpact: A coach reviewing a meaningful free-text metric like a pain-location description sees a truncated, useless fragment and must hover each cell one-by-one (impossible on touch) to read what the athlete actually wrote.
findings.recommendation: For text metrics, allow wrap or a wider/expandable cell, or render text custom metrics as a separate stacked list under the table rather than forcing them into the fixed-width numeric grid. At minimum, raise the truncation length substantially and add a click-to-expand. Avoid lossy display of athlete-reported truth.

verdicts.reasoning: Every cited fact checks out against the real code, and the path is the default/only one (not theoretical). (1) CustomMetricField.tsx:63-70 renders text metrics in a `<textarea rows={2}>` with NO maxLength — athletes can enter arbitrarily long free text; commit() stores it verbatim as value_text (line 37). (2) LogWeekOverview.tsx:588 hard-truncates non-numeric values via `String(v).slice(0, 14)` for display. (3) The full value is exposed only through the cell title tooltip at LogWeekOverview.tsx:578 (`${row.label}: ${v}`), which is hover-only and inaccessible on touch. (4) The 'only coach review surface' claim holds: OtherMetricsTable (LogWeekOverview.tsx:480, rendered at 262) is the sole place custom metrics are shown to the coach. LogModeView.tsx:273-280 feeds enabled custom defs exclusively into LogWeekOverview; LogDayCard renders no custom metrics (grep for custom_metric/value_text/CustomMetric in LogDayCard.tsx returned zero hits); the only other value_text usages (TodayScreen.tsx, SessionHeader.tsx) are athlete-facing entry, not coach review. (5) Text rows get `text-left` amber styling and the Avg column shows '—' (numericValues empty when !row.numeric, LogWeekOverview.tsx:556-559,591-592) — matching 'left-aligned amber cells with no Avg.' (6) WeekMetricsSettings.tsx:334 explicitly suggests 'Mood, Hydration, Pain area' as example custom metrics — exactly the free-text categories whose values routinely exceed 14 chars (e.g. 'left shoulder, front delt' → 'left shoulder,'). Reachability is the common case, not an edge case: any text-type custom metric value > 14 chars is truncated in the coach's only at-a-glance review. Severity held at medium: it is a degraded-display defect (the athlete's value is stored intact, recoverable via desktop hover) rather than data loss or a crash, but it makes athlete-reported truth effectively unreadable on the coach's primary review surface and entirely unreadable on touch, which is more than cosmetic.
